notes.anchor: our life is set up as a target for all the weapons of fortune
notes.note: The commonplace (consolatio pervulgata) that to be born human is to be exposed to every blow of Fortune (fortuna). This stock opening of the consolation genre tells the mourner that nothing new or unjust has befallen him.

notes.anchor: happiest who have not raised children
notes.note: Cicero's distinctively political consolation: in the ruin of the commonwealth (the 52 BC crisis of mob violence after Clodius's death) those who lost children are less wretched than they would be had they lost them with the republic still standing. The argument turns private grief into a comment on the times.

notes.anchor: there is no evil in death
notes.note: Nihil mali esse in morte: the philosophical dilemma Cicero borrows --- if perception survives death it is immortality, not death; if perception is lost there is no one left to suffer. The argument, common to Epicurean and Platonic consolation, is developed at length in his Tusculan Disputations book 1.

notes.anchor: not wait for the medicine of time
notes.note: Temporis medicina: the consolation that lapse of time heals the sharpest grief. Cicero urges that reason and forethought (consilio, ratione) should anticipate at once what time would eventually bring of itself.
